core_takeaway: Agent_1 narrowly beat Agent_5 because Jewel was anchored early and is worth slightly more than Agent_5's Stone, but both positions secured their top item.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1's first move helped make Jewel-1 a stable anchor, yet Agent_5 also locked Stone-5; the final 1.62 gap mainly follows from 42 vs 40 value under the final-round discount.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by making Jewel the organizing demand, and later public turns repeatedly converged on the same baseline: Jewel-1, Quill-2, Pencil-3, Apple-4, Stone-5. Agent_5 was not excluded; its top target Stone became part of the public baseline.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available. Private thoughts in all rounds describe stable anchors and a narrow Apple hedge. Agent_1 privately protected Jewel and Apple; Agent_5 privately protected Stone and accepted small Apple/Pencil concessions only if Stone stayed safe.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 had four baseline proposals plus Agent_5's two-agent bundle, Round 2 mixed the baseline with deviations, and Round 3 all five formal proposals were identical to the final baseline.
phase_notes.private_voting: Available. Early private votes rejected the baseline when agents saw richer immediate bundles, but by Round 3 every voter accepted the identical baseline because it preserved core anchors and minimized churn.
phase_notes.reflection: Available. Reflections diagnose stable anchors, Apple as the low-risk balancing lever, and Stone/Pencil as risks if moved; they explain consensus as anchor preservation rather than a clean last- or first-speaker dominance effect.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to declare Jewel as the central target, then recast later proposals around Jewel staying with Agent_1. That anchor survived every round and became part of the final unanimous allocation.

evidence.quote: Baseline Step 1 allocation: Jewel to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Last position also secured its primary anchor
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5's late-speaking position did not leave it marginalized. It repeatedly demanded Stone, the group baseline gave Stone to Agent_5, and Agent_5 accepted the final plan because Stone stayed with it.

evidence.quote: Stone is my clear top target (value 40).
Stone to Agent_5 aligns with my top priority

mechanisms.theme: Final margin comes from top-item value difference
mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation gives each of Agent_1 and Agent_5 one major anchor. Agent_1's Jewel value is 42 while Agent_5's Stone value is 40, so the final-round discounted utilities differ by 1.62, matching the recorded first-minus-last gap.

mechanisms.theme: Early utility chasing delayed, rather than confirmed, first-position dominance
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's own baseline did not immediately win: Agent_1 and Agent_2 rejected early baseline proposals when Agent_5's bundle offered them more utility, while agents left with no items rejected that bundle. Consensus required abandoning those richer deviations and returning to the shared anchor set.

evidence.quote: Gives me Jewel only (42) versus Proposal 5's Jewel+Apple (80).
Gives me nothing (no items); zero utility for me

mechanisms.theme: Anchor-preserving reflections converted disagreement into consensus
mechanisms.explanation: The reflections did not recommend giving Agent_1 extra items; they recommended preserving every core anchor and using only Apple as a narrow hedge. That process protected both Agent_1's Jewel and Agent_5's Stone, limiting any first-position edge.

evidence.quote: Keep Step 1 as the active move
Use Step 2 as a minimal, collaborative balance lever
